howItWorks.intro: Footings support structures by distributing weight into the ground. Calculating the right amount of concrete depends on the footing shape, size, and quantity.
rules: Round footings (piers): π × (diameter/2)² × depth. Convert all measurements to feet first
Rectangular footings: length × width × depth. Typical wall footings are twice the wall width
Footings must extend below the frost line — check your local building code for the required depth
howItWorks.outro: For deck piers and post footings, round Sonotube forms are most common. For wall footings and strip footings, rectangular forms are standard. Always add 10% extra concrete for waste and spillage.

faq.question: How deep should footings be?
faq.answer: Footings must extend below the frost line, which ranges from 12 inches in southern states to 48+ inches in northern states. Check your local building code. Typical depths: deck footings 36-48 inches, fence posts 24-36 inches, house footings per engineer specs.

faq.question: What size footing do I need for a deck?
faq.answer: Most deck footings use 12-inch diameter Sonotube forms set 42 inches deep. For heavy loads or tall decks, 16-inch or 18-inch diameter may be required. Check your local building code — most require a permit and specify minimum footing sizes for decks.

faq.question: How much concrete for a fence post hole?
faq.answer: A typical fence post hole (8 inches diameter, 36 inches deep) uses about 0.3 cubic feet of concrete — roughly one 50-lb bag per post. For a 6-foot privacy fence, you need a post every 6-8 feet, so a 100-foot fence needs about 13-17 posts.

faq.question: Can I mix my own concrete for footings?
faq.answer: For a few small footings, bagged concrete mix is practical. For larger jobs (over 1 cubic yard), ordering ready-mix is faster, stronger, and often cheaper. One 80-lb bag yields about 0.6 cubic feet of concrete.

faq.question: Do footings need rebar?
faq.answer: Most building codes require at least 2 pieces of #4 rebar in wall footings and 1 piece of rebar extending up from pier footings into the post bracket. Rebar prevents cracking and ties the footing to the structure above. Always follow your local code.

faq.question: What is the difference between a footing and a pier?
faq.answer: A footing is a wider concrete base that spreads the load over more soil — typically used under walls and foundations. A pier is a round or square column of concrete (often formed with a Sonotube) that carries point loads, like deck posts. Many structures use both: a footing at the bottom with a pier extending up.
